comment: Sample configuration for the code-scan workflow (workflows/code-scan.js). For interactive use (asks for URL + branch conversationally), use the 'code-scan' skill instead — these examples are for the headless/batch invocation.

modes.comment: mode picks between a full analysis and a cheap rescan of the findings already on record. Default is 'auto'.
modes.auto: Rescan if the repo already has an analysis/ folder with pending findings; otherwise run a full scan. The default.
modes.full: Always run the full analysis, ignoring any prior analysis/. Discovers new issues and rewrites the reports — which also discards the fix statuses a rescan recorded, since findings are regenerated from scratch.
modes.rescan: Only re-verify the findings already on record and write their status back to the same JSON/CSV. Finds NO new issues. Fails loudly rather than falling back to a full scan when there is no prior analysis to rescan.

example_1_single_repo.description: Scan a single repo on its main branch. A first run has no analysis/ folder, so auto mode runs a full scan.

example_2_multiple_repos.description: Scan several repos in one run — each gets independent stack detection, so a pure-EDS repo never triggers the Java analyzer and vice versa

example_3_trusted_mode.description: Skip the setup/analysis confirmation gates — for CI or batch sweeps where you already trust the target list

example_4_custom_location.description: Override the default clone location (defaults to <ai-agent-repo>/repos)

example_5_rescan.description: Re-verify the findings from a previous scan against the latest code and write Fixed/Open/Partially Fixed status back into the same analysis/*-analysis-findings.json and *-analysis-issues.csv. Reads only the files carrying a known finding, so it costs a fraction of a full scan.

example_6_rescan_ci_sprint_check.description: Typical CI/scheduled use: after each sprint, recheck every tracked repo's open findings and refresh the trackers. trustedMode skips the gates; batchSize tunes how many issues each verifier agent handles at once (default 12 — lower it for dense files, raise it for shallow findings).

example_7_rescan_regression_check.description: Rescans skip issues already marked Fixed/Not Applicable — re-reading a deleted file every run costs tokens for no signal. recheckFixed re-verifies those too, catching a fix that got reverted. Worth running before a release, not every sprint.

example_8_force_full_rescan_of_scanned_r.description: Force a fresh full analysis of a repo that already has an analysis/ folder — the right call after a large merge or refactor, where a rescan would faithfully verify the old findings while missing everything the new code introduced. Note this regenerates findings from scratch, discarding recorded fix statuses.

outputs.comment: Everything lands in analysis/ at the root of the CLONED repo (<baseDir>/<repoName>/analysis/), never inside this toolkit.
rescan: analysis/<domain>-analysis-findings.json — same file, updated in place: each issue gains status, statusDetail, verifiedFile/verifiedLine, firstSeenCommit, lastVerifiedCommit/Date, fixedInCommit; a scanHistory entry is appended per run
analysis/<domain>-analysis-issues.csv — same file, rebuilt with the status columns, sorted open-work-first
analysis/rescan-summary.md — cross-domain status table, regressions, still-open list
analysis/.verify/ — scratch: the batch plan + per-batch verdicts (audit trail; purged and rewritten at the start of each rescan)
outputs.statuses: Open · Fixed · Partially Fixed · Not Applicable (the code carrying it no longer exists) · Unverifiable (the verifier couldn't tell — routed to a human, never silently closed)
